facade_colour: buff/cream coloured brick with polychromatic accents

hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a late-nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a pair, they retain most of their original building features, including shared roofline and mirrored facades featuring brick cladding with decorative brickwork, flat headed window and door openings, and projecting bay windows set under gables with decorative woodwork. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District. Summary: Reference Primary Address Entrance Address(es) Number 345. 85 Oxford Street N/A 346. 87 Oxford Street N/A 183 DCH | FO :D FO YTIC 5202 88 Oxford Street 88 OXFORD STREET

context.street_character: One-way | Residential street | Tree-lined | Heritage character

photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: buff/cream coloured brick (not stone); distinctive light-coloured polychromatic brick with darker accents. Full-height bay window with veranda/porch at ground level.
photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Well-maintained; light-coloured stone/brick facade in good condition
photo_observations.window_details: Ground floor: 2 windows in projecting bay. Second floor: 2 windows in bay. Bay window spans both floors. Small gable window at attic level. Veranda with turned posts at ground level.
photo_observations.bay_window_details: Full-height two-storey canted bay window spanning ground and second floors. Three-sided with segmental-arched openings.
photo_observations.porch_details: Full-width veranda with turned wooden posts and decorative brackets. Copper or verdigris-coloured metal roof over veranda.
photo_observations.chimney_details: Single chimney visible at roof ridge.
photo_observations.decorative_details: Light-coloured stone/brick facade is distinctive; iron fence in front
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Green/teal painted trim; iron fence; generally well preserved
photo_observations.notes: Facade material corrected from stone to brick. This is buff/cream brick (also called white or yellow brick), common in Toronto Victorian architecture.

notes: Distinctive buff/cream building at corner. Bay-and-Gable form visible.

facade_detail.composition: The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its semi-detached bay-and-gable house-form typology.
facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using mixed, and 1 principal entrance.
facade_detail.heritage_summary: Constructed as a pair, they retain most of their original building features, including shared roofline and mirrored facades featuring brick cladding with decorative brickwork, flat headed window and door openings, and projecting bay windows set under gables with decorative woodwork. 85 Oxford Street N/A 346.

facade_description: Semi-detached two-storey bay-and-gable in buff/cream coloured brick with polychromatic accents under a cross-gable roof. Features include a double-height three-sided projecting bay and simple bargeboards. Green/teal painted trim; iron fence; generally well preserved. Pre-1889 construction.
heritage_summary: Constructed as a pair, they retain most of their original building features, including shared roofline and mirrored facades featuring brick cladding with decorative brickwork, flat headed window and door openings, and projecting bay windows set under gables with decorative woodwork. 85 Oxford Street N/A 346.
